architecture.summary: Inference-Time Intervention (ITI) identifies sparse sets of attention heads with high linear probing accuracy for truthfulness, then shifts their output activations along truth-correlated directions during autoregressive decoding. A trained linear probe per head discovers the principal separating hyperplane between true and false statements in activation space; during inference, the model's residual stream is perturbed by a scaled truthful direction vector at selected heads in each layer, repeated autoregressively. No model weights are trained; only probes are fitted to frozen model activations.
trained_components: linear probes (one per attention head per layer)

biblio.title: Inference-Time Intervention: Eliciting Truthful Answers from a Language Model

claims.statement: On an instruction-finetuned LLaMA called Alpaca, ITI improves its truthfulness from 32.5% to 65.1%
ITI locates truthful directions using only few hundred examples
On LLaMA-7B with mass mean shift direction at optimal α=20, true*informative score reaches 42.3% compared to baseline 30.5%
On Vicuna, ITI improves true*informative from 51.5% to 74.0%
Zero-shot transfer: on MMLU, LLaMA-7B + ITI achieves 40.16% compared to baseline 35.71%
ITI identifies a trade-off between truthfulness and helpfulness that can be balanced by tuning the intervention strength parameter α
The technique is minimally invasive and computationally inexpensive, requiring only addition of a single constant vector per layer with close to zero computational overhead
LLMs may have an internal representation of the likelihood of something being true in intermediate activations, even as they produce falsehoods on the surface
